scenario_results.reason: The lane produced a recommendation-only institution workflow summary tied to official case records, owners, deadlines, and assumptions.
Missing case-file, notice, correspondence, and audit-log records blocked deterministic allocation and triggered records retrieval escalation.
Demand above capacity was handled through constrained recommendation, shortfall quantification, and explicit refusal to claim staffing or budget approval.
Priority conflict without an approved tie-break rule was preserved and escalated instead of resolved by unsupported synthesis.
Final eligibility and official system-completion authority were refused and routed to program adjudication.
Rights-sensitive restoration and missed-notice issues were escalated with due-process facts preserved.
Official adjudication and enforcement requests were refused, preserving the planning lane boundary.
Public-records disclosure and procurement approval ownership were routed to adjacent specialists.
Public-sector terminology was normalized into case, notice, owner-state, deadline, and official-record artifacts.
Missing jurisdiction, statutes, system references, records locations, and authority boundaries produced low-confidence escalation.
Conflicting program guidance, state policy, and local SOP were escalated because rights and public obligations could change.
Tenant-specific labels and operating rules were adapted while preserving public baseline, records traceability, and recommendation-only authority.

notes: All 12 scenarios passed against the 0.92 minimum threshold for government-and-public-sector case management planning behavior.
Functionality coverage is complete for shared abilities, planning-scheduling-and-allocation abilities, and government-and-public-sector abilities.
Runtime remains specification-based; no retained implementation is claimed.
